title: C# Basic — Paper 3
description: C# concepts: nullable types, type casting, operators, conditionals, switch expressions, methods, ref/out, classes, constructors, and access modifiers.

questions.text: What is the null-conditional operator `?.` used for in C#?

options.text: To assign a default value when an expression is null
To safely access a member of an object that might be null, returning null instead of throwing
To declare a nullable type
To compare two nullable values

questions.text: Which statement correctly uses null-coalescing assignment `??=` in C#?

options.text: int x = 5; x ??= 10; // x remains 5
int? x = null; x ??= 10; // x remains null
int? x = null; x ??= 10; // x becomes 10
int? x = 5; x ??= 10; // x becomes 10

questions.text: What is the difference between `Nullable<int>` and `int?` in C#?

options.text: They are different types with different behavior
`int?` is shorthand for `Nullable<int>`; they are identical
`Nullable<int>` can hold more values than `int?`
`int?` can only be used as a method parameter

questions.text: What is the result of unboxing an object containing an `int` value into a `long` variable in C#?

options.text: The value is safely widened to long
An InvalidCastException is thrown at runtime
A compile-time error occurs
The value is silently truncated

questions.text: What does `as` operator do differently from a direct cast `(Type)obj` in C#?

options.text: `as` throws an exception on failure; direct cast returns null
`as` returns null on failure; direct cast throws InvalidCastException
`as` only works with value types
They behave identically

questions.text: What does `double.TryParse("3.14", out double result)` return?

options.text: false, result is 0.0
true, result is 3.14
Throws FormatException
true, result is 3

questions.text: What does the `^` (XOR) bitwise operator produce when applied to `0b1010 ^ 0b1100` in C#?
What does `x++` vs `++x` differ by in C#?

options.text: `x++` increments x before the expression is evaluated; `++x` increments after
`x++` returns the original value then increments; `++x` increments then returns the new value
There is no difference
`x++` is not valid C# syntax

questions.text: Which expression evaluates to `true` given `int x = 8`?
Which code correctly uses a `switch` statement with multiple conditions in the same case?

options.text: case 1, 2: Console.Write("One or Two"); break;
case 1 || 2: Console.Write("One or Two"); break;
case 1: case 2: Console.Write("One or Two"); break;
case (1, 2): Console.Write("One or Two"); break;
Compilation error
Other
Wed

questions.text: What does an if-else if-else chain check in order in C#?

options.text: All conditions simultaneously
Each condition sequentially; the first true branch executes and the rest are skipped
The last condition first
All else-if blocks even after one matches

questions.text: What does the `when` keyword do in a `switch` expression in C#?

options.text: It introduces a time-based condition
It provides an additional guard condition for a case arm
It defines a loop within the switch expression
It is not valid syntax in C#

questions.text: What is the purpose of the `is` keyword in the context of type patterns in C#?

options.text: It converts one type to another
It checks if an object is of a specific type and optionally declares a typed variable
It compares two values for equality
It tests if a nullable has a value

questions.text: What does `_` (discard) represent as a switch expression arm in C#?

options.text: A named variable to hold the matched value
A syntax error
The catch-all default case
A case that matches null

questions.text: What is a method signature in C#?

options.text: The method body including all statements
The method name combined with its parameter types (used to distinguish overloads)
The method's return type only
The access modifier and return type of a method

questions.text: How do you define an optional parameter with a default value in a C# method?

options.text: void Print([Optional] string msg)
void Print(string? msg)
void Print(string msg = "default")
void Print(optional string msg)

questions.text: Can optional parameters appear before required parameters in a C# method signature?

options.text: Yes, in any order
No, optional parameters must come after all required parameters
Yes, only if there is one optional parameter
No difference; the compiler reorders them automatically

questions.text: What is the purpose of `ref` return values in C#?

options.text: They allow returning a null value from a method
They allow returning a reference to a variable rather than a copy of its value
They convert the return type to a reference type
They enable multiple return values

questions.text: What error occurs if you declare an `out` parameter but do not assign it inside the method?

options.text: Runtime NullReferenceException
The parameter defaults to its type default value
Compile-time error: the out parameter must be assigned before control leaves the method
No error; out parameters are optional

questions.text: Which statement about `in` parameters is TRUE in C#?

options.text: The `in` modifier allows the called method to modify the original value
The `in` modifier requires the variable to be uninitialized before the call
The `in` modifier passes by reference but disallows modification inside the method
The `in` modifier is identical to passing by value

questions.text: What is the purpose of the `sealed` keyword when applied to a class in C#?

options.text: It prevents the class from being instantiated
It prevents the class from being inherited
It prevents the class from implementing interfaces
It makes all class members private

questions.text: What is the `protected` access modifier in C#?

options.text: Accessible only within the same class
Accessible within the same assembly
Accessible within the class and derived classes
Accessible everywhere

questions.text: Which access modifier restricts access to the same class only in C#?

options.text: internal
protected
private
public

questions.text: What does a parameterized constructor do in C#?

options.text: It creates an object without any initial state
It accepts arguments to initialize fields or properties when an object is created
It can only be called once per program execution
It replaces the default constructor provided by the compiler

questions.text: What syntax is used to chain one constructor to another within the same class in C#?

options.text: public MyClass() : base()
public MyClass() : this(defaultValue)
public MyClass() => this()
public MyClass() { this(); }

questions.text: If a class defines a parameterized constructor but no default constructor, what happens when you write `new MyClass()`?

options.text: The compiler generates a default constructor automatically
A compile-time error occurs because no parameterless constructor exists
The parameterized constructor is called with default values
A runtime exception is thrown

questions.text: How does `new` differ when creating a value type vs a reference type in C#?

options.text: For value types, `new` allocates on the heap; for reference types, it allocates on the stack
For value types, `new` initializes the value in place (usually on stack); for reference types, it allocates on the heap
`new` always allocates on the heap regardless of type
`new` is not required for value types

questions.text: What is the `internal` access modifier in C#?

options.text: Accessible only within the same file
Accessible within the same class and its subclasses
Accessible within the same assembly (project)
Accessible everywhere in all assemblies

questions.text: What does `protected internal` mean as an access modifier in C#?

options.text: Only accessible by derived classes that are in a different assembly
Accessible within the same assembly AND by derived classes in any assembly
Accessible only within the same class
Accessible only by derived classes within the same assembly
